stage-m34.text: What does CVE-2023-20198 let an unauthenticated attacker do on Cisco IOS XE?
options: Create a privilege-15 account by sending a crafted HTTP request to the Web UI
stage-m34.explanation: A crafted Web UI request created a full privilege-15 local account without any authentication.

stage-m34.text: What CVSS score did CVE-2023-20198 receive?
options: 10.0 — no authentication, no user interaction, complete compromise
stage-m34.explanation: It scored the maximum 10.0: unauthenticated, no interaction, total device compromise.

stage-m34.text: What does privilege level 15 mean in Cisco IOS?
options: Full administrative control of the device
A read-only observer with no config rights
stage-m34.explanation: Privilege 15 is the highest level — full administrative control, not read-only.

stage-m34.text: Which commands disable the IOS XE HTTP management interface to remove the attack surface?
options: no ip http server / no ip http secure-server
stage-m34.explanation: Disabling the HTTP/HTTPS server removes the vulnerable Web UI entirely.

stage-m34.text: By Cisco's first advisory on October 16, 2023, roughly how many IOS XE devices were already implanted?
stage-m34.explanation: Mass exploitation had already implanted over 50,000 devices before the advisory.

stage-m34.text: Why was exposing the IOS XE Web UI to the internet so dangerous here?
options: It gave unauthenticated attackers a direct path to full device takeover
stage-m34.explanation: An internet-reachable management UI with a pre-auth flaw equals remote full compromise.

stage-m34.text: What is the broader lesson for management interfaces from CVE-2023-20198?
options: Never expose device management UIs to the internet; restrict and disable when unused
stage-m34.explanation: Keeping management planes off the public internet is a core hardening principle.

stage-m34.text: After CVE-2023-20198, what is a key indicator of compromise to hunt for?
options: Unexpected new privilege-15 local accounts on the device
stage-m34.explanation: Attacker-created privilege-15 accounts are the hallmark of this exploitation.

stage-m36.text: What did the ArcaneDoor campaign target?
options: Cisco ASA and FTD perimeter security appliances
stage-m36.explanation: ArcaneDoor compromised the very perimeter firewalls meant to defend the network.

stage-m36.text: Which ArcaneDoor malware provided persistence surviving reboots and upgrades?
options: Line Runner — installed as a malicious ASDM plugin in the plugin directory
stage-m36.explanation: Line Runner persisted as a rogue ASDM plugin, surviving reboots and software updates.

stage-m36.text: Is the Line Dancer in-memory implant easily detected with standard logs and 'show users'?
options: No — it is memory-resident and evades standard log analysis and 'show' commands
stage-m36.explanation: Line Dancer ran in memory and was invisible to ordinary device commands and logs.

stage-m36.text: Which inspection most reliably detects a Line Runner plugin implant?
options: dir disk0:*.zip — unexpected ZIP archives in the plugin directory are the indicator
stage-m36.explanation: Line Runner appears as unexpected ZIP files in the plugin directory, surfaced by a directory listing.

stage-m36.text: Who was the ArcaneDoor threat actor (UAT4356 / STORM-1849) attributed to?
options: A nation-state with advanced zero-day development capability
stage-m36.explanation: ArcaneDoor was a sophisticated nation-state espionage operation using zero-days.

stage-m36.text: Why do nation-state actors target perimeter firewalls like ASA/FTD?
options: They see all traffic, sit at the edge, and often lack endpoint-style monitoring
stage-m36.explanation: Edge security appliances offer a powerful, under-monitored vantage point for espionage.

stage-m36.text: What defensive practice helps catch implants like Line Runner/Line Dancer?
options: File integrity verification against signed baselines and off-device telemetry
stage-m36.explanation: Comparing against signed baselines and centralizing telemetry exposes tampering the device hides.

stage-m36.text: Why is abusing a legitimate plugin mechanism effective for persistence?
options: The implant loads through a trusted, expected path and survives updates
stage-m36.explanation: Riding a trusted plugin path gives durable, low-suspicion persistence across reboots and upgrades.

stage-m37.text: CVE-2018-0171 exploits the Cisco Smart Install protocol on which port, and what auth does it need?
options: TCP/4786, and it requires no valid credentials
stage-m37.explanation: Smart Install listens on TCP/4786 and the buffer overflow needed no authentication.

stage-m37.text: Which U.S. agencies attributed mass CVE-2018-0171 exploitation to Russian state actor APT28 (GRU)?
options: FBI and CISA, in joint Technical Alert TA18-106A
stage-m37.explanation: FBI and CISA issued TA18-106A attributing the activity to APT28 / the GRU.

stage-m37.text: Why was Smart Install Client such a widespread risk?
options: It was enabled by default on many IOS/IOS XE devices even when unused for provisioning
It had to be manually enabled
stage-m37.explanation: Default-on Smart Install left huge numbers of devices exposed on TCP/4786 unnecessarily.

stage-m37.text: What IOS command disables the Smart Install Client to eliminate the attack surface?
stage-m37.explanation: 'no vstack' disables the Smart Install Client, closing TCP/4786.

stage-m37.text: At peak exposure, how many devices had TCP/4786 reachable from the internet?
stage-m37.explanation: Researchers found over 250,000 internet-exposed Smart Install endpoints at peak.

stage-m37.text: Why did the lack of authentication make CVE-2018-0171 so impactful?
options: Any internet host could trigger the overflow with no credentials
stage-m37.explanation: Unauthenticated exploitation on a default-on internet-facing port enabled mass attacks.

stage-m37.text: What hardening principle does CVE-2018-0171 reinforce?
options: Disable unused default-on services and block their ports at the edge
stage-m37.explanation: Turning off unneeded default services (and blocking TCP/4786) removes the attack surface.

stage-m37.text: How would a defender quickly assess exposure to CVE-2018-0171?
options: Scan for devices with TCP/4786 reachable and Smart Install enabled
stage-m37.explanation: Identifying reachable TCP/4786 with Smart Install active pinpoints vulnerable devices.
